changes: OpenClaw still did not cut a newer stable release than v2026.3.13-1, but its official showcase remains the most important fresh strategic signal: the visible workflow surface is now much broader across home automation, visual briefings, accounting intake, job search, support automation, and hardware control.
ZeroClaw shipped stable v0.5.4 on 2026-03-20 and immediately followed with new beta builds, reinforcing the Rust lightweight lane as one of the fastest-moving branches in the field.
IronClaw moved again with v0.21.0 on 2026-03-20, keeping the privacy / defense-in-depth branch on a very fast cadence instead of settling after v0.19.0.
TinyAGI released v0.0.16 and HiClaw released v1.0.8 on 2026-03-20, so the orchestration / manager-worker lane is still shipping actively across multiple implementations.

Poco v0.3.2, PicoClaw nightly + v0.2.3, Hermes Agent v2026.3.17, AstrBot v4.20.1, and SwarmClaw v1.2.4 remain very recent, so the broader ecosystem is still in a high-churn release window rather than a pause.
whyItMatters: The biggest category signal is still real-world workflow breadth, not just GitHub churn: OpenClaw's showcase is increasingly evidence that messaging-native assistants are escaping demo-land and touching recurring operational work.
ZeroClaw, PicoClaw, and Poco keep validating the market demand for lighter, cheaper, and more productized alternatives to the reference implementation.
IronClaw and NanoClaw keep the security-first critique alive, which matters because runtime trust is becoming a primary product axis rather than a niche concern.
TinyAGI, HiClaw, and SwarmClaw show that agent teams, control planes, and manager-worker structures are becoming normalized branches of the category.
The monitored set and top-level taxonomy still hold. This is ecosystem deepening and release velocity, not a strategic map reset.
